{{Short description|Psychological mechanisms against disease}}
The '''behavioral immune system''' is a phrase coined by the psychological scientist [[Mark Schaller]] to refer to a suite of psychological mechanisms that allow individual organisms to detect the potential presence of infectious [[parasite]]s or [[pathogen]]s in their immediate environment, and to engage in behaviors that prevent contact with those objects and individuals.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1207/s15327965pli1702_2 | vauthors = Schaller M  |year=2006 |title=Parasites, behavioral defenses, and the social psychological mechanisms through which cultures are evoked |journal=Psychological Inquiry |volume=17 |issue=2 |pages= 96–101|s2cid=219729311 }}</ref><ref name="sd2007">{{cite book | vauthors = Schaller M, Duncan LA |title=Evolution and the social mind: Evolutionary psychology and social cognition | veditors = Forgas JP, Haselton MG, von Hippel W |publisher=Psychology Press |location=New York |year=2007 |pages=293–307 |chapter=The behavioral immune system: Its evolution and social psychological implications}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1177/0963721411402596 | vauthors = Schaller M, Park JH |year=2011 |title=The behavioral immune system (and why it matters) |journal=Current Directions in Psychological Science |volume=20 |issue=2 |pages=99–103|citeseerx=10.1.1.459.1601 |s2cid=39930315 }}</ref><ref>{{Citation |last1=Thornhill |first1=Randy |title=The Parasite-Stress Theory of Sociality and the Behavioral Immune System |date=2015 |work=Evolutionary Perspectives on Social Psychology |pages=419–437 |editor-last=Zeigler-Hill |editor-first=Virgil |url=https://link.springer.com/10.1007/978-3-319-12697-5_32 |access-date=2025-08-04 |place=Cham |publisher=Springer International Publishing |language=en |doi=10.1007/978-3-319-12697-5_32 |isbn=978-3-319-12696-8 |last2=Fincher |first2=Corey L. |editor2-last=Welling |editor2-first=Lisa L. M. |editor3-last=Shackelford |editor3-first=Todd K.|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

The existence of a behavioral immune system has been documented across many animal species, including humans. It is theorized that the mechanisms that comprise the behavioral immune system evolved as a crude first line of defense against disease-causing pathogens.<ref name="sd2007"/>

In humans and animals, activating a physiological immune response to pathogens is effective, but metabolically costly. Immune responses are activated at the expense of other fitness enhancing activities.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Dantzer R | title = Cytokine-induced sickness behavior: where do we stand? | journal = Brain, Behavior, and Immunity | volume = 15 | issue = 1 | pages = 7–24 | date = March 2001 | pmid = 11259077 | doi = 10.1006/brbi.2000.0613 | s2cid = 3187017 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Hart BL | title = Biological basis of the behavior of sick animals | journal = Neuroscience and Biobehavioral Reviews | volume = 12 | issue = 2 | pages = 123–37 | date = June 1988 | pmid = 3050629 | doi = 10.1016/s0149-7634(88)80004-6 | s2cid = 17797005 }}</ref> Inflammation after infection can also be harmful to the body (e.g., contribute to diseases of aging).<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Conner EM, Grisham MB | title = Inflammation, free radicals, and antioxidants | journal = Nutrition | volume = 12 | issue = 4 | pages = 274–7 | date = April 1996 | pmid = 8862535 | doi = 10.1016/s0899-9007(96)00000-8 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Khansari N, Shakiba Y, Mahmoudi M | title = Chronic inflammation and oxidative stress as a major cause of age-related diseases and cancer | journal = Recent Patents on Inflammation & Allergy Drug Discovery | volume = 3 | issue = 1 | pages = 73–80 | date = January 2009 | pmid = 19149749 | doi = 10.2174/187221309787158371 }}</ref> In addition to cultural adaptations to avoid pathogens, the behavioral immune system acts as a set of defense mechanisms to protect against pathogens before infection occurs <ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Murray DR, Schaller M | chapter = The Behavioral Immune System|date=2016 | title = Advances in Experimental Social Psychology| volume = 53|pages=75–129|publisher=Elsevier|isbn=978-0-12-804737-8 |doi= 10.1016/bs.aesp.2015.09.002 }}</ref><ref name="Schaller 99–103">{{cite journal| vauthors = Schaller M, Park JH |date=April 2011|title=The Behavioral Immune System (and Why It Matters) |journal=Current Directions in Psychological Science|volume=20|issue=2|pages=99–103|doi=10.1177/0963721411402596|s2cid=39930315|issn=0963-7214}}</ref><ref name="Schaller_2011">{{cite journal | vauthors = Schaller M | title = The behavioural immune system and the psychology of human sociality | journal = Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London. Series B, Biological Sciences | volume = 366 | issue = 1583 | pages = 3418–26 | date = December 2011 | pmid = 22042918 | pmc = 3189350 | doi = 10.1098/rstb.2011.0029 }}</ref>
[[File:Quick_simple_diagram_of_the_behavioral_immune_system.png|thumb|Shows pathogens and non-infection diseases that create similar cues connoting presence of pathogens, real or perceived, which activate the behavioral immune system]]

== Proximate mechanisms ==
Mechanisms for the behavioral immune system include sensory processes through which cues connoting the presence of parasitic infections are perceived (e.g., the smell of a foul odor, the sight of [[Acne scarring|pox]] or [[pustules]]), as well as [[stimulus–response]] systems through which these sensory cues trigger a cascade of aversive affective, cognitive, and behavioral reactions (e.g., arousal of [[disgust]], automatic activation of cognitions that connote the threat of disease, behavioral avoidance).<ref name="sd2007" /><ref name="Disgust as a disease-avoidance mech">{{cite journal | vauthors = Oaten M, Stevenson RJ, Case TI | title = Disgust as a disease-avoidance mechanism | journal = Psychological Bulletin | volume = 135 | issue = 2 | pages = 303–21 | date = March 2009 | pmid = 19254082 | doi = 10.1037/a0014823 }}</ref>

=== Sensory components ===
Early and current research on behavioral immune system activation has been focused on visual cues or triggers that elicit responses. However, recent work suggests that other sensory modalities may be at work for disease detection.<ref name="Murray_2019">{{cite journal | vauthors = Murray DR, Prokosch ML, Airington Z | title = PsychoBehavioroimmunology: Connecting the Behavioral Immune System to Its Physiological Foundations | journal = Frontiers in Psychology | volume = 10 | article-number = 200 | date = 2019-02-07 | pmid = 30804853 | pmc = 6378957 | doi = 10.3389/fpsyg.2019.00200 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

==== Smell ====
Studies show that olfactory cues of disease elicit disgust and predict [[pathogen avoidance]] behaviors.<ref name="Murray_2019" /> In humans, body odors from diseased individuals are rated less desirable and likeable,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Regenbogen C, Axelsson J, Lasselin J, Porada DK, Sundelin T, Peter MG, Lekander M, Lundström JN, Olsson MJ | display-authors = 6 | title = Behavioral and neural correlates to multisensory detection of sick humans | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America | volume = 114 | issue = 24 | pages = 6400–6405 | date = June 2017 | pmid = 28533402 | pmc = 5474783 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.1617357114 | bibcode = 2017PNAS..114.6400R | doi-access = free }}</ref> and perceived as unhealthier, more intense, and less pleasant.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Olsson MJ, Lundström JN, Kimball BA, Gordon AR, Karshikoff B, Hosseini N, Sorjonen K, Olgart Höglund C, Solares C, Soop A, Axelsson J, Lekander M | display-authors = 6 | title = The scent of disease: human body odor contains an early chemosensory cue of sickness | journal = Psychological Science | volume = 25 | issue = 3 | pages = 817–23 | date = March 2014 | pmid = 24452606 | doi = 10.1177/0956797613515681 | s2cid = 14530226 }}</ref> Disgust of body odors is also influenced by the closeness of the source of odor. Odors from family members are rated as less disgusting than body odors from strangers.<ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Stevenson RJ, Repacholi BM |date=2005|title=Does the source of an interpersonal odour affect disgust? A disease risk model and its alternatives |journal=European Journal of Social Psychology|volume=35|issue=3|pages=375–401|doi=10.1002/ejsp.263|issn=0046-2772}}</ref> Experimental studies showed that the presence of aversive odors leads to more prophylactic behaviors, such as more willing condom use<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Tybur JM, Bryan AD, Magnan RE, Hooper AE | title = Smells like safe sex: olfactory pathogen primes increase intentions to use condoms | journal = Psychological Science | volume = 22 | issue = 4 | pages = 478–80 | date = April 2011 | pmid = 21350181 | doi = 10.1177/0956797611400096 | s2cid = 31099982 }}</ref> and less-direct prophylactic behaviors, like stricter punishment for moral violations.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Schnall S, Haidt J, Clore GL, Jordan AH | title = Disgust as embodied moral judgment | journal = Personality & Social Psychology Bulletin | volume = 34 | issue = 8 | pages = 1096–109 | date = August 2008 | pmid = 18505801 | pmc = 2562923 | doi = 10.1177/0146167208317771 }}</ref>

A new body of work has linked olfactory ''acuity'' to disgust and behavioral immune system responses. Given that olfactory cues for pathogen threat are often ambiguous and overgeneralized, a better ability to detect smells would lead to better pathogen avoidance.<ref name="Murray_2019" /> Recent work suggests that higher olfactory acuity may be associated with higher avoidance motivation.<ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Fay AJ, Bovier ER |date=2018-02-24|title=Testing a Curvilinear Relationship between Chemosensory Responsivity and Avoidance Motivation |journal=Adaptive Human Behavior and Physiology|volume=4|issue=2|pages=207–222|doi=10.1007/s40750-018-0088-1|s2cid=148883832|issn=2198-7335}}</ref>

==== Taste ====
Gustatory stimuli, particularly of spoiled foods, elicit feels of disgust and motivate pathogen avoidance behavior.<ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Tybur JM, Laakasuo M, Ruff J, Klauke F |date=September 2016|title=How pathogen cues shape impressions of foods: the omnivore's dilemma and functionally specialized conditioning |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|volume=37|issue=5|pages=376–386|doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2016.03.002|bibcode=2016EHumB..37..376T |issn=1090-5138}}</ref> People with more disgust sensitivity are predicted to have more aversion to novel or foreign foods.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Al-Shawaf L, Lewis DM, Alley TR, Buss DM | title = Mating strategy, disgust, and food neophobia | journal = Appetite | volume = 85 | pages = 30–5 | date = February 2015 | pmid = 25450899 | doi = 10.1016/j.appet.2014.10.029 | s2cid = 14110094 | hdl = 11693/22547 | hdl-access = free }}</ref>

==== Sight and touch ====
Visual cues of pathogen threat have been linked to increases in tactile sensitivity and lead to perceived people with accents as more foreign, especially among individuals with higher disgust sensitivity.<ref name="Reid 471–478">{{cite journal| vauthors = Reid SA, Zhang J, Anderson GL, Gasiorek J, Bonilla D, Peinado S |date=September 2012|title=Parasite primes make foreign-accented English sound more distant to people who are disgusted by pathogens (but not by sex or morality) |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|volume=33|issue=5|pages=471–478|doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2011.12.009|bibcode=2012EHumB..33..471R |issn=1090-5138}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Hunt DF, Cannell G, Davenhill NA, Horsford SA, Fleischman DS, Park JH | title = Making your skin crawl: The role of tactile sensitivity in disease avoidance | journal = Biological Psychology | volume = 127 | pages = 40–45 | date = July 2017 | pmid = 28478137 | doi = 10.1016/j.biopsycho.2017.04.017 | s2cid = 7894884 | url = https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/files/119765634/MakingYourSkinCrawl_ms_unformatted.pdf }}</ref> Disgust sensitivity among individuals is also predictive of preferred amount of personal space.<ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Park JH |date=August 2015|title=Introversion and human-contaminant disgust sensitivity predict personal space |journal=Personality and Individual Differences|volume=82|pages=185–187|doi=10.1016/j.paid.2015.03.030|issn=0191-8869|url=https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/files/35514950/Park2015PAID.pdf|hdl=1983/aa8c5c1c-f834-432d-9885-b5d44fff50d4|hdl-access=free}}</ref>

==== Overgeneralization ====
The "[[Detection theory|smoke detector principle]]"<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Haselton MG, Nettle D | title = The paranoid optimist: an integrative evolutionary model of cognitive biases | journal = Personality and Social Psychology Review | volume = 10 | issue = 1 | pages = 47–66 | date = February 2006 | pmid = 16430328 | doi = 10.1207/s15327957pspr1001_3 | s2cid = 5725102 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Nesse RM | title = The smoke detector principle: Signal detection and optimal defense regulation | journal = Evolution, Medicine, and Public Health | volume = 2019 | issue = 1 | page = 1 | date = 2018-12-04 | pmid = 30697424 | pmc = 6343816 | doi = 10.1093/emph/eoy034 }}</ref> of evolved systems that regulate protective responses has also been used to describe the behavioral immune systems tendency to overgeneralize.<ref name="Ackerman_2018">{{cite journal| vauthors = Ackerman JM, Hill SE, Murray DR |date=2018-01-26|title=The behavioral immune system: Current concerns and future directions |journal=Social and Personality Psychology Compass|volume=12|issue=2|article-number=e12371|doi=10.1111/spc3.12371|issn=1751-9004|doi-access=free|hdl=2027.42/142457|hdl-access=free}}</ref> Evolved responses to signals of pathogen threat cannot be perfect and rely on liberal identification criteria.<ref name="Ackerman_2018" /> This makes the behavioral immune system susceptible to activating when pathogens are absent. Noninfectious physical and mental abnormalities including elderly appearance,<ref name="Duncan_2009b">{{cite journal| vauthors = Duncan LA, Schaller M |date=October 27, 2009|title=Prejudicial Attitudes Toward Older Adults May Be Exaggerated When People Feel Vulnerable to Infectious Disease: Evidence and Implications |journal=Analyses of Social Issues and Public Policy|volume=9|issue=1|pages=97–115|doi=10.1111/j.1530-2415.2009.01188.x }}</ref><ref name="Miller_2012">{{cite journal | vauthors = Miller SL, Maner JK | title = Overperceiving disease cues: the basic cognition of the behavioral immune system | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 102 | issue = 6 | pages = 1198–213 | date = June 2012 | pmid = 22329656 | doi = 10.1037/a0027198 }}</ref> disabilities,<ref name="Schaller_2003">{{cite journal| vauthors = Schaller M, Park J, Faulkner J |date=January 2003|title=Prehistoric dangers and contemporary prejudices |journal=European Review of Social Psychology|volume=14|issue=1|pages=105–137|doi=10.1080/10463280340000036|s2cid=30987296|issn=1046-3283}}</ref> obesity,<ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Lund EM, Miller SL |date=July 2014|title=Is obesity un-American? Disease concerns bias implicit perceptions of national identity |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|volume=35|issue=4|pages=336–340|doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2014.03.004|bibcode=2014EHumB..35..336L |issn=1090-5138}}</ref> and disfigurement<ref name="Ackerman_2009">{{cite journal | vauthors = Ackerman JM, Becker DV, Mortensen CR, Sasaki T, Neuberg SL, Kenrick DT | title = A pox on the mind: Disjunction of attention and memory in the processing of physical disfigurement | journal = Journal of Experimental Social Psychology | volume = 45 | issue = 3 | pages = 478–485 | date = May 2009 | pmid = 19578547 | pmc = 2699287 | doi = 10.1016/j.jesp.2008.12.008 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Miller SL, Maner JK | title = Sick body, vigilant mind: the biological immune system activates the behavioral immune system | journal = Psychological Science | volume = 22 | issue = 12 | pages = 1467–71 | date = December 2011 | pmid = 22058109 | doi = 10.1177/0956797611420166 | s2cid = 206585861 }}</ref> can act as cues of pathogen presence, when none are present. Even though many false alarms may be triggered in response to these nonharmful cues, the costs associated with behavioral immune activation may be relatively small to the costs of missing a true disease threat.<ref name="Ackerman_2018" />
:{| class="wikitable"
|-
!
! Respond "Absent"
! Respond "Present"
|-
! Stimulus Present
| [[Type 1 error#Type I and type II errors|Miss]]
| Hit
|-
! Stimulus Absent
| Correct Rejection
| [[Type 1 error#Type I and type II errors|False Alarm]]
|} Table taken from signal detection theory (SDT). Cues, such as foul smell, that activate the behavioral immune system, such as the emotion of disgust, can have stimulus present (real pathogen threat) or absent (perceived pathogen threat).

=== Responses ===

==== Disgust ====
The pathogen disgust system and the behavioral immune system have been studied separately, but there is recognition that they are functionally the same.<ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Lieberman D, Patrick C |date=2014|title=Are the behavioral immune system and pathogen disgust identical? |journal=Evolutionary Behavioral Sciences|volume=8|issue=4|pages=244–250|doi=10.1037/ebs0000018|issn=2330-2933}}</ref>  Darwin first recognized that the emotion of [[disgust]] aided in avoiding "tainted" food.<ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Darwin C | veditors = Darwin F | chapter = Disdain — Contempt — Disgust — Guilt — Pride, Etc. — Helplessness — Patience — Affirmation and Negation | title = The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals|year=2009|pages=265–292|place=Cambridge|publisher=Cambridge University Press|doi=10.1017/cbo9780511694110.013|isbn=978-0-511-69411-0 }}</ref> The emotion of disgust has now been recognized as an adaptive function for avoiding pathogen exposure in response to cues of potential pathogen threat.  However, disgust to pathogen-related cues should be context-dependent to function adaptively.<ref name="Cepon-Robins_2021">{{cite journal | vauthors = Cepon-Robins TJ, Blackwell AD, Gildner TE, Liebert MA, Urlacher SS, Madimenos FC, Eick GN, Snodgrass JJ, Sugiyama LS | display-authors = 6 | title = Pathogen disgust sensitivity protects against infection in a high pathogen environment | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America | volume = 118 | issue = 8 | article-number = e2018552118 | date = February 2021 | pmid = 33597300 | pmc = 7923589 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.2018552118 | bibcode = 2021PNAS..11818552C | doi-access = free }}</ref> In one study on the [[Shuar]], an indigenous subsistence-based population with high-pathogen stress, pathogen disgust sensitivity (PDS), measured with a disgust questionnaire, was used to predict pathogen infection. The study found that individual PDS was negatively correlated with pathogen infection predictive of the hypothesis that disgust acts as a pathogen defense mechanism sensitive to local costs and benefits of avoidance and infection.<ref name="Cepon-Robins_2021" />

==== Functional flexibility ====
Functional flexibility is a term used to describe the ability of the behavioral immune system to adjust responses to pathogens depending on the individual's infection-related threat and infection-relating vulnerability. Like many evolved threat management systems, the behavioral immune system is sensitive to the costs and benefits of pathogen avoidance.<ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Schaller M, Neuberg SL | chapter = Danger, Disease, and the Nature of Prejudice(s)|date=2012 |title = Advances in Experimental Social Psychology| volume = 46|pages=1–54|publisher=Elsevier|isbn=978-0-12-394281-4 |doi=10.1016/b978-0-12-394281-4.00001-5}}</ref><ref name="Schaller 99–103"/><ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Schaller M, Park JH, Kenrick DT |date=2007-04-05|title=Human evolution and social cognition |journal=Oxford Handbooks Online|pages=491–504 |doi=10.1093/oxfordhb/9780198568308.013.0033|isbn=978-0-19-856830-8 }}</ref>

==== Reactive and proactive ====
Two categories of outputs can be generated by the behavioral immune system; reactive and proactive responses<ref name="Ackerman_2018" /> Reactive responses occur in response to the presence of cues connoting an immediate infection risk. The responses generally take the form of avoidant or [[prophylactic]] behaviors.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Curtis V, de Barra M, Aunger R | title = Disgust as an adaptive system for disease avoidance behaviour | journal = Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London. Series B, Biological Sciences | volume = 366 | issue = 1563 | pages = 389–401 | date = February 2011 | pmid = 21199843 | pmc = 3013466 | doi = 10.1098/rstb.2011.0002 }}</ref><ref name="Tybur_2013">{{cite journal | vauthors = Tybur JM, Lieberman D, Kurzban R, DeScioli P | title = Disgust: evolved function and structure | journal = Psychological Review | volume = 120 | issue = 1 | pages = 65–84 | date = January 2013 | pmid = 23205888 | doi = 10.1037/a0030778 }}</ref> These can include restricted sexual attitudes,<ref name = "Duncan_2009">{{cite journal | vauthors = Duncan LA, Schaller M, Park JH | title = Perceived vulnerability to disease: Development and validation of a 15-item self-report instrument. | journal = Personality and Individual Differences | date = October 2009 | volume = 47 | issue = 6 | pages = 541–6 |doi=10.1016/j.paid.2012.08.021 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Murray DR, Jones DN, Schaller M |date=January 2013|title=Perceived threat of infectious disease and its implications for sexual attitudes |journal=Personality and Individual Differences|volume=54|issue=1|pages=103–108|doi=10.1016/j.paid.2012.08.021|issn=0191-8869}}</ref><ref name="Schaller_2008">{{cite journal | vauthors = Schaller M, Murray DR | title = Pathogens, personality, and culture: disease prevalence predicts worldwide variability in sociosexuality, extraversion, and openness to experience | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 95 | issue = 1 | pages = 212–21 | date = July 2008 | pmid = 18605861 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.95.1.212 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.476.4335 }}</ref> positivity towards condom use,<ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Tybur JM, Bryan AD, Lieberman D, Hooper AE, Merriman LA |date=August 2011|title=Sex differences and sex similarities in disgust sensitivity |journal=Personality and Individual Differences|volume=51|issue=3|pages=343–348|doi=10.1016/j.paid.2011.04.003|issn=0191-8869}}</ref> and avoidance of people with cues associated with illness.<ref name="Schaller_2003" /><ref name="Park_2007">{{cite journal| vauthors = Park JH, Schaller M, Crandall CS |date=November 2007|title=Pathogen-avoidance mechanisms and the stigmatization of obese people |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|volume=28|issue=6|pages=410–414|doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2007.05.008|bibcode=2007EHumB..28..410P |issn=1090-5138}}</ref>

Proactive responses occur in response to the long-term and persistent threat of pathogens.<ref name="Ackerman_2018" /> Across species, proactive management of pathogen threat can be seen in hygiene behaviors to mitigate reoccurring bacterial and viral threats.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Curtis V, Biran A | title = Dirt, disgust, and disease. Is hygiene in our genes? | journal = Perspectives in Biology and Medicine | volume = 44 | issue = 1 | pages = 17–31 | date = 2001 | pmid = 11253302 | doi = 10.1353/pbm.2001.0001 | s2cid = 15675303 }}</ref> Additionally, proactive responses can be seen in the importance placed on a potential mate's [[physical attractiveness]], symmetry, and secondary sex-characteristics which are all indicators of health, and healthy people are less likely to carry disease.<ref name="Gangestad_1993">{{cite journal| vauthors = Gangestad SW, Buss DM |date=March 1993|title=Pathogen prevalence and human mate preferences |journal=Ethology and Sociobiology|volume=14|issue=2|pages=89–96|doi=10.1016/0162-3095(93)90009-7|hdl=2027.42/30928|issn=0162-3095|hdl-access=free}}</ref><ref name="Hill_2015">{{cite journal | vauthors = Hill SE, Prokosch ML, DelPriore DJ | title = The impact of perceived disease threat on women's desire for novel dating and sexual partners: is variety the best medicine? | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 109 | issue = 2 | pages = 244–61 | date = August 2015 | pmid = 26030057 | doi = 10.1037/pspi0000024 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Jones BC, Feinberg DR, Watkins CD, Fincher CL, Little AC, DeBruine LM |date=2012-10-23|title=Pathogen disgust predicts women's preferences for masculinity in men's voices, faces, and bodies |journal=Behavioral Ecology|volume=24|issue=2|pages=373–379|doi=10.1093/beheco/ars173|issn=1465-7279|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name="Lee_2011">{{cite journal | vauthors = Lee AJ, Zietsch BP | title = Experimental evidence that women's mate preferences are directly influenced by cues of pathogen prevalence and resource scarcity | journal = Biology Letters | volume = 7 | issue = 6 | pages = 892–5 | date = December 2011 | pmid = 21697166 | pmc = 3210674 | doi = 10.1098/rsbl.2011.0454 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Hugenberg K, Young SG, Sacco DF, Bernstein MJ |date=2011-07-28|title=Social Categorization Influences Face Perception and Face Memory |journal=Oxford Handbooks Online|doi=10.1093/oxfordhb/9780199559053.013.0013}}</ref>

== Influences on social psychology ==
Two social consequences of activating the behavioral immune system according to Ackerman et al., are "1. increased aversion and avoidance of unfamiliar and outgroup targets and 2. strengthened cohesion with familiar and ingroup targets."<ref name="Ackerman_2018" />

=== Interpersonal perception ===
[[Interpersonal perception]] is influenced by the behavioral immune system due to the early stage cognitive processes recruited for identifying pathogenic threat and the risks associated with interpersonal contagion.

Visual attention is one these early cognitive process recruited toward cues of pathogen threat and perceivers find it more difficult to visually disengage from faces possessing these cues, like physical abnormalities, even if no threat is present .<ref name="Ackerman_2009" />

The behavioral immune system may also cause people who perceive greater pathogen threat to report greater distinctions between ingroup and outgroup members, and classify strangers as more threatening.<ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Makhanova A, Miller SL, Maner JK |date=January 2015|title=Germs and the out-group: Chronic and situational disease concerns affect intergroup categorization. |journal=Evolutionary Behavioral Sciences|volume=9|issue=1|pages=8–19|doi=10.1037/ebs0000028|issn=2330-2933}}</ref><ref name="Miller_2012"/><ref name="Reid 471–478"/>

=== Judgment and decision-making ===
Judgments, inferences, and decisions about people and objects are other cognitive processes recruited by the behavioral immune system that are affected by pathogen threat. These processes motivative choices that help perceivers avoid unfamiliar stimuli that may include contamination dangers.<ref>{{Citation|title=Pollution and Purity in Moral and Political Judgment |work=Advances in Experimental Moral Psychology|year=2014|publisher=Bloomsbury Academic|doi=10.5040/9781472594150.ch-006|isbn=978-1-4725-0938-3 |last1=Inbar |first1=Yoel |last2=Pizarro |first2=David |pages=111–129 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Peng M, Chang L, Zhou R |date=March 2013|title=Physiological and behavioral responses to strangers compared to friends as a source of disgust |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|volume=34|issue=2|pages=94–98|doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2012.10.002|bibcode=2013EHumB..34...94P |issn=1090-5138}}</ref>

Examples of the influence perceived pathogen threat has on these processes include: avoidance of controllable risk,<ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Prokosch ML, Gassen J, Ackerman JM, Hill SE |date=October 2019|title=Caution in the time of cholera: Pathogen threats decrease risk tolerance. |journal=Evolutionary Behavioral Sciences|volume=13|issue=4|pages=311–334|doi=10.1037/ebs0000160|s2cid=92163234 |issn=2330-2933}}</ref> reduced desire for social affiliation,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Sacco DF, Young SG, Hugenberg K | title = Balancing competing motives: adaptive trade-offs are necessary to satisfy disease avoidance and interpersonal affiliation goals | journal = Personality & Social Psychology Bulletin | volume = 40 | issue = 12 | pages = 1611–23 | date = December 2014 | pmid = 25278107 | doi = 10.1177/0146167214552790 | s2cid = 8082873 }}</ref> and devaluation of consumer products previously handled by strangers.<ref name="Huang 430–443">{{cite journal| vauthors = Huang JY, Ackerman JM, Newman GE |date=October 2017|title=Catching (Up with) Magical Contagion: A Review of Contagion Effects in Consumer Contexts |journal=Journal of the Association for Consumer Research|volume=2|issue=4|pages=430–443|doi=10.1086/693533|s2cid=51791428|issn=2378-1815}}</ref>

Additional lines of research on the behavioral immune system have shown that people engage in more reticent and conservative forms of behavior under conditions in which they feel more vulnerable to disease transmission. For instance, when the potential threat of disease is made salient, people tend to be less extraverted or sociable.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Mortensen CR, Becker DV, Ackerman JM, Neuberg SL, Kenrick DT | title = Infection breeds reticence: the effects of disease salience on self-perceptions of personality and behavioral avoidance tendencies | journal = Psychological Science | volume = 21 | issue = 3 | pages = 440–7 | date = March 2010 | pmid = 20424082 | doi = 10.1177/0956797610361706 | hdl-access = free | s2cid = 2791764 | hdl = 1721.1/67706 }}</ref>

=== Close relationships ===
The behavioral immune system influences potential mating choices and sexual behavior. High concern for chronic pathogen threats and environments with greater pathogen stress increase the value an individual places on physical attractiveness,<ref name="Gangestad_1993" /><ref name="Lee_2011" /><ref name="White 2429–2436">{{cite journal | vauthors = White AE, Kenrick DT, Neuberg SL | title = Beauty at the ballot box: disease threats predict preferences for physically attractive leaders | journal = Psychological Science | volume = 24 | issue = 12 | pages = 2429–36 | date = December 2013 | pmid = 24121414 | doi = 10.1177/0956797613493642 | s2cid = 20502884 }}</ref> and lessens preference for mates with physical abnormality or sex-divergent features.<ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Lee AJ, Brooks RC, Potter KJ, Zietsch BP |date=November 2015|title=Pathogen disgust sensitivity and resource scarcity are associated with mate preference for different waist-to-hip ratios, shoulder-to-hip ratios, and body mass index |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|volume=36|issue=6|pages=480–488|doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2015.07.002|bibcode=2015EHumB..36..480L |hdl=1893/28703|issn=1090-5138|hdl-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Little AC, DeBruine LM, Jones BC | title = Exposure to visual cues of pathogen contagion changes preferences for masculinity and symmetry in opposite-sex faces | journal = Proceedings. Biological Sciences | volume = 278 | issue = 1714 | pages = 2032–9 | date = July 2011 | pmid = 21123269 | pmc = 3107643 | doi = 10.1098/rspb.2010.1925 }}</ref> Among perceivers, cues of pathogen prevalence (real or perceived)<ref name="Ackerman_2018" /> are associated with more restricted sexual pursuits and attitudes to avoid infection.<ref name="Duncan_2009" /><ref name="Schaller_2008" /> Opposite, more sexual opportunism is found in individuals with perceived insufficient immune systems for survival in environments with higher pathogen stress.<ref name="Hill_2015" />

=== Stereotyping and prejudice ===
[[File:Banner Against Xenophobia.jpg|thumb|Xenophobia is defined by Oxford languages as: dislike of or prejudice against people from other countries. Some studies indicate behavioral immune activation may lead to greater in-group preference and prejudice towards out-groups.]]
Another outcome of behavioral immune system activity is prejudice and stereotyping of outgroup members. Individuals at higher risk of pathogen infection are more likely to stigmatize other individuals possessing cues of disease, real or perceived.<ref name="Park_2007" /><ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Navarrete CD, Fessler DM |date=July 2006|title=Disease avoidance and ethnocentrism: the effects of disease vulnerability and disgust sensitivity on intergroup attitudes |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|volume=27|issue=4|pages=270–282|doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2005.12.001|bibcode=2006EHumB..27..270N |issn=1090-5138}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Mortensen CR, Becker DV, Ackerman JM, Neuberg SL, Kenrick DT | title = Infection breeds reticence: the effects of disease salience on self-perceptions of personality and behavioral avoidance tendencies | journal = Psychological Science | volume = 21 | issue = 3 | pages = 440–7 | date = March 2010 | pmid = 20424082 | doi = 10.1177/0956797610361706 | s2cid = 2791764 | hdl = 1721.1/67706 | hdl-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Huang JY, Sedlovskaya A, Ackerman JM, Bargh JA | title = Immunizing against prejudice: effects of disease protection on attitudes toward out-groups | journal = Psychological Science | volume = 22 | issue = 12 | pages = 1550–6 | date = December 2011 | pmid = 22058107 | doi = 10.1177/0956797611417261 | s2cid = 18413566 | hdl = 1721.1/77620 | hdl-access = free }}</ref><ref name="Faulkner_2004">{{cite journal| vauthors = Faulkner J, Schaller M, Park JH, Duncan LA |year=2004|title=Evolved Disease–Avoidance Mechanisms and Contemporary Xenophobic Attitudes|journal=Group Processes and Intergroup Relations|volume=7|issue=4|pages=333–53|citeseerx=10.1.1.1018.9323|doi=10.1177/1368430204046142|s2cid=28558930}}</ref><ref name="Miller_2012" /><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Crandall CS, Moriarty D | title = Physical illness stigma and social rejection | journal = The British Journal of Social Psychology | volume = 34 ( Pt 1) | issue = 1 | pages = 67–83 | date = March 1995 | pmid = 7735733 | doi = 10.1111/j.2044-8309.1995.tb01049.x }}</ref>

The disease–avoidant processes that characterize the behavioral immune system have been shown to contribute to prejudices against [[obese]] individuals, [[elderly]] individuals, and people with physical disfigurements or [[disabilities]].<ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Park JH, Faulkner J, Schaller M |date=June 2003|title=Evolved disease-avoidance processes and contemporary anti-social behavior: Prejudicial attitudes and avoidance of people with physical disabilities|journal=Journal of Nonverbal Behavior|volume=27|issue=2|pages=65–87|doi=10.1023/A:1023910408854|s2cid=41615132|issn=1573-3653}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Park J, Schaller M, Crandall CS |year=2007|title=Pathogen-avoidance mechanisms and the stigmatization of obese people|url=http://www.psych.ubc.ca/~schaller/ParkSchallerCrandall2007.pdf|journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|volume=28|issue=6|pages=410–4|doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2007.05.008 |bibcode=2007EHumB..28..410P }}</ref><ref name="Duncan_2009b" /> In addition, the behavioral immune system appears to contribute to [[xenophobia]] and [[ethnocentrism]].<ref name="Faulkner_2004" /><ref name="Navarrete_2007">{{cite journal| vauthors = Navarrete CD, Fessler D, Eng S |year=2007|title=Elevated ethnocentrism in the first trimester of pregnancy|url=http://www.sscnet.ucla.edu/anthro/faculty/fessler/pubs/NavarretePregEthnocentr2007.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080513013908/http://www.sscnet.ucla.edu/anthro/faculty/fessler/pubs/NavarretePregEthnocentr2007.pdf|archive-date=May 13, 2008|journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|volume=28|issue=1|pages=60–5|doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2006.06.002 |bibcode=2007EHumB..28...60N }}</ref> This can be seen among pregnant women, which face higher infection-related vulnerability, that express an increase ethnocentric views.<ref name="Navarrete_2007" /> These attitudes might function as a way to avoid people with new pathogens or practices that local practices are unsuited to manage.<ref name="Ackerman_2018" /> One implication is that these prejudices tend to be exaggerated under conditions in which people feel especially vulnerable to the potential transmission of infectious diseases.

=== Group processes and cultural norms ===
The behavioral immune system has the ability to impact group intragroup attitudes and behaviors. Research shows that pathogen stress is associated with higher social conformity<ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Murray DR, Schaller M |date=2011-11-20|title=Threat(s) and conformity deconstructed: Perceived threat of infectious disease and its implications for conformist attitudes and behavior |journal=European Journal of Social Psychology|volume=42|issue=2|pages=180–188|doi=10.1002/ejsp.863|issn=0046-2772}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Wu BP, Chang L |date=July 2012|title=The social impact of pathogen threat: How disease salience influences conformity |journal=Personality and Individual Differences|volume=53|issue=1|pages=50–54|doi=10.1016/j.paid.2012.02.023|issn=0191-8869}}</ref> and higher levels of disgust, which aids in pathogen avoidance<ref name="Huang 430–443"/><ref name="Disgust as a disease-avoidance mech"/><ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Haidt J, McCauley C, Rozin P |date=1994|title=Disgust Scale |website=PsycTESTS Dataset|doi=10.1037/t12177-000}}</ref><ref name="Tybur_2013" /> and predictive of greater sensitivity to moral violations.<ref name="Ackerman_2018" />

Some studies have used the behavioral immune system to explain the root of more fundamental dimensions of culture including the variance in: individualism/collectivism,<ref name="Fincher_2008">{{cite journal | vauthors = Fincher CL, Thornhill R, Murray DR, Schaller M | title = Pathogen prevalence predicts human cross-cultural variability in individualism/collectivism | journal = Proceedings. Biological Sciences | volume = 275 | issue = 1640 | pages = 1279–85 | date = June 2008 | pmid = 18302996 | pmc = 2602680 | doi = 10.1098/rspb.2008.0094 }}</ref> social and political orientation,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Murray DR, Schaller M, Suedfeld P | title = Pathogens and politics: further evidence that parasite prevalence predicts authoritarianism | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 8 | issue = 5 | article-number = e62275 | date = 2013-05-01 | pmid = 23658718 | pmc = 3641067 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0062275 | bibcode = 2013PLoSO...862275M | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Terrizzi JA, Shook NJ, McDaniel MA |date=March 2013|title=The behavioral immune system and social conservatism: a meta-analysis |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|volume=34|issue=2|pages=99–108|doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2012.10.003|bibcode=2013EHumB..34...99T |s2cid=11812927 |issn=1090-5138}}</ref><ref name="White 2429–2436"/> and religious beliefs,<ref name="Fincher 2587–2594">{{cite journal | vauthors = Fincher CL, Thornhill R | title = Assortative sociality, limited dispersal, infectious disease and the genesis of the global pattern of religion diversity | journal = Proceedings. Biological Sciences | volume = 275 | issue = 1651 | pages = 2587–94 | date = November 2008 | pmid = 18664438 | pmc = 2605802 | doi = 10.1098/rspb.2008.0688 }}</ref><ref name="Fincher_2012">{{cite journal | vauthors = Fincher CL, Thornhill R | title = Parasite-stress promotes in-group assortative sociality: the cases of strong family ties and heightened religiosity | journal = The Behavioral and Brain Sciences | volume = 35 | issue = 2 | pages = 61–79 | date = April 2012 | pmid = 22289223 | doi = 10.1017/s0140525x11000021 | s2cid = 54624893 | url = http://eprints.gla.ac.uk/77679/1/77679.pdf }}</ref> in response to levels of pathogen stress.

Collectivist cultures defined by behavioral manifestations such as ethnocentrism and social conformity which aid in pathogen avoidance, have been correlated with higher historical pathogen stress compared to individualistic cultures.<ref name="Fincher_2008" /> Given that there might be benefits associated with individualistic cultures in the societies they create, individualist cultures also confer greater pathogen exposure. In environments with greater pathogen stress, the behavioral manifestations of collectivism that help avoid pathogens may serve an adaptive advantage.<ref name="Fincher_2008" />

=== Critiques of in-group preference ===
In-group preferences defined by the degree to which people prefer interacting with and investing in family, friends, and in group members has been suggested to be a function of the behavioral immune system in order to defend against pathogens.<ref name="Fincher_2008" /><ref name="Schaller_2011" /><ref name="Fincher_2012" /> However, these studies on cross-population level data have been criticized for not incorporating non-independence variables and alternative hypotheses.<ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Thornhill R, Fincher CL |date=July 2013|title=Commentary on Hackman, J., & Hruschka, D. (2013). Fast life histories, not pathogens, account for state-level variation in homicide, child maltreatment, and family ties in the U.S. Evolution and Human Behavior, 34,118–124 |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|volume=34|issue=4|pages=314–315|doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2013.03.006|bibcode=2013EHumB..34..314T |issn=1090-5138}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Van de Vliert E, Postmes T | title = Climato-economic livability predicts societal collectivism and political autocracy better than parasitic stress does | journal = The Behavioral and Brain Sciences | volume = 35 | issue = 2 | pages = 94–5 | date = April 2012 | pmid = 22289160 | doi = 10.1017/s0140525x11001075 | s2cid = 13449531 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Currie TE, Mace R | title = Analyses do not support the parasite-stress theory of human sociality | journal = The Behavioral and Brain Sciences | volume = 35 | issue = 2 | pages = 83–5 | date = April 2012 | pmid = 22289294 | doi = 10.1017/s0140525x11000963 | s2cid = 302233 | url = http://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/1339505/1/download7.pdf }}</ref> In a follow up study, using measures the same measure for in-group preference, Hofstede's collectivism, Van de Vliert's [[in-group favoritism]], and Fincher and Thornhill's strength of family ties, find that less government effectiveness is a better predictor of in-group preference than pathogen stress.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Hruschka DJ, Henrich J | title = Institutions, parasites and the persistence of in-group preferences | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 8 | issue = 5 | article-number = e63642 | date = 2013-05-21 | pmid = 23704926 | pmc = 3660589 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0063642 | bibcode = 2013PLoSO...863642H | doi-access = free }}</ref>

==Implications for immunology==

=== Complimentary ===
Some research suggests that the behavioral immune system has implications for the functioning of the physiological [[immune system]] (PIS) too. One study found that the mere [[visual perception]] of diseased-looking people stimulated white blood cells to respond more aggressively to infection (as indicated by the production of the proinflammatory [[cytokine]] [[Interleukin 6]] in response to a bacterial stimulus).<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Schaller M, Miller GE, Gervais WM, Yager S, Chen E | title = Mere visual perception of other people's disease symptoms facilitates a more aggressive immune response | journal = Psychological Science | volume = 21 | issue = 5 | pages = 649–52 | date = May 2010 | pmid = 20483842 | doi = 10.1177/0956797610368064 | s2cid = 58423 }}</ref>

In other studies, exposure to visual environmental pathogen cues, in addition to increased feelings of disgust and prejudice responses associated with the behavioral immune system (BIS), upregulate oral and blood immune inflammatory biomarkers.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Stevenson RJ, Hodgson D, Oaten MJ, Barouei J, Case TI | title = The effect of disgust on oral immune function | journal = Psychophysiology | volume = 48 | issue = 7 | pages = 900–7 | date = July 2011 | pmid = 21166686 | doi = 10.1111/j.1469-8986.2010.01165.x }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Stevenson RJ, Hodgson D, Oaten MJ, Moussavi M, Langberg R, Case TI, Barouei J | title = Disgust elevates core body temperature and up-regulates certain oral immune markers | journal = Brain, Behavior, and Immunity | volume = 26 | issue = 7 | pages = 1160–8 | date = October 2012 | pmid = 22841694 | doi = 10.1016/j.bbi.2012.07.010 | s2cid = 34237534 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Stevenson RJ, Hodgson D, Oaten MJ, Sominsky L, Mahmut M, Case TI |date=2015-07-01|title=Oral Immune Activation by Disgust and Disease-Related Pictures |journal=Journal of Psychophysiology|volume=29|issue=3|pages=119–129|doi=10.1027/0269-8803/a000143|issn=0269-8803}}</ref> This body of literature suggests that visual cues connected to the BIS may invoke PIS responses when pathogen threat is immediate.<ref name="Murray_2019" />

=== Compensatory ===
In the absence of an immediate pathogen threat, the PIS and BIS may not be complimentary, but compensatory. For example, in an experiment that stimulated release of proinflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-1b, TNF-alpha) collected from healthy individuals, were not related to self-reported germ aversion, but in-vivo IL-6 levels were negatively correlated to germ aversion and perceived longevity. This research may highlight the function of the BIS for long-term health by decreasing proinflammatory responses (function of IL-6), linked to diseases of aging.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Gassen J, Prokosch ML, Makhanova A, Eimerbrink MJ, White JD, Proffitt Leyva RP, Peterman JL, Nicolas SC, Reynolds TA, Maner JK, McNulty JK, Eckel LA, Nikonova L, Brinkworth JF, Phillips MD, Mitchell JB, Boehm GW, Hill SE | display-authors = 6 | title = Behavioral immune system activity predicts downregulation of chronic basal inflammation | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 13 | issue = 9 | article-number = e0203961 | date = 2018-09-20 | pmid = 30235317 | pmc = 6147464 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0203961 | bibcode = 2018PLoSO..1303961G | doi-access = free }}</ref>

Research also indicates that immune-relevant interventions which target [[pathogen transmission]] can interrupt behavioral responses. For example, receiving a flu vaccination or washing one's hands can reduce the extent of negative interpersonal and intergroup attitudes elicited by disease cues and concerns.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Huang JY, Sedlovskaya A, Ackerman JM, Bargh JA | title = Immunizing against prejudice: effects of disease protection on attitudes toward out-groups | journal = Psychological Science | volume = 22 | issue = 12 | pages = 1550–6 | date = December 2011 | pmid = 22058107 | doi = 10.1177/0956797611417261 | hdl-access = free | s2cid = 18413566 | hdl = 1721.1/77620 }}</ref>

== Sickness behavior ==
The large body of literature on the behavioral immune system is focused on behaviors triggered by pathogen cues in the environment and the role disgust plays in mitigating exposure ''before'' infection. However, the emotion associated with being sick, ''lassitude'', that is triggered by an active infection may also act as behavioral defense mechanism against pathogens. One study suggests that lassitude may help fight against an active infection by:

* reducing energetically expensive movement to make more energy available to the immune system.<ref name="Schrock_2020">{{cite journal| vauthors = Schrock JM, Snodgrass JJ, Sugiyama LS |date=January 2020|title=Lassitude: The emotion of being sick |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|volume=41|issue=1|pages=44–57|doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2019.09.002|bibcode=2020EHumB..41...44S |s2cid=203480861 |issn=1090-5138}}</ref>
* reducing exposure to additional infections and injuries that would increase the immune systems' workload.<ref name="Schrock_2020" />
* promoting thermoregulatory behaviors that facilitate immunity.<ref name="Schrock_2020" />
* regulating food consumption to be beneficial for the host but detrimental to pathogens.<ref name="Schrock_2020" />
* deploying strategies that elicit caregiving behavior from social allies.<ref name="Schrock_2020" />

== COVID-19 ==
The behavioral immune system's prediction of ingroup favoritism and ethnocentric beliefs has been applied to individual beliefs during the global pandemic, COVID-19. In several studies, individuals who scored higher in dispositional worry about disease reported stronger preference for restrictive travel bans on several regions of Asia. One study found that as COVID-19 cases increased, individuals reported higher concern about contracting the virus and showed greater levels of [[Right-Wing Authoritarianism]], including conservatism and traditionalism.<ref name="paid111081">{{cite journal | vauthors = Pazhoohi F, Kingstone A | title =Associations of political orientation, xenophobia, right-wing authoritarianism, and concern of COVID-19: Cognitive responses to an actual pathogen threat | journal = Personality and Individual Differences | volume =  111081 | date = 2021 | article-number =111081 | doi = 10.1016/j.paid.2021.111081 | pmid =36540871 | pmc = 9756797 }}</ref> Experimental increase of pandemic salience also increased support of travel bans on high risk nation (China and Italy), but not on low risk nations like Canada and Mexico.<ref name="pmid33903849">{{cite journal | vauthors = Moran JB, Goh JX, Kerry N, Murray DR | title = Outbreaks and Outgroups: Three Tests of the Relationship Between Disease Avoidance Motives and Xenophobia During an Emerging Pandemic | journal = Evolutionary Psychological Science | volume = 7| issue = 4| pages = 419–429 | date = April 2021 | pmid = 33903849 | pmc = 8060690 | doi = 10.1007/s40806-021-00283-z }}</ref> Another study measuring individual perceived vulnerability to disease (PVD) and responses to COVID-19 found that higher PVD was positively associated with stronger reactions to the threat of COVID-19, including increased anxiety, perceptions that people should alter their typical behavior, and self-reported importance of [[social distancing]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Makhanova A, Shepherd MA | title = Behavioral immune system linked to responses to the threat of COVID-19 | journal = Personality and Individual Differences | volume = 167 | page = 110221 | date = December 2020 | pmid = 32834281 | pmc = 7328593 | doi = 10.31234/osf.io/6dq3g }}</ref>

== References ==

* [http://discovermagazine.com/2009/mar/17-is-patriotism-a-subconscious-way-for-humans-to-avoid-disease "Is Patriotism a Subconscious Way for Humans to Avoid Disease," ''Discover'' magazine]
* [http://www.newsweek.com/id/130623 "You Can Blame the Bugs," ''Newsweek'' magazine]
